Teaching in Higher Education - Project outline - Mercedes Vargas

Presenting Prep

Gestalt Principles for Scientific Figures in Agricultural Sciences

Length: 50 minutes
Audience: Mixed undergrad & grad - agricultural sciences
Materials: Slide deck + figure clinic handout

Learning outcomes

1
Identify at least five Gestalt principles and explain how each influences the perception of scientific figures
2
Diagnose visual problems in real agricultural figures using Gestalt principles as a critical framework
3
Propose and justify at least one concrete visual improvement to a flawed figure using perceptual reasoning

Backstage agenda — 50 min

0:00 – 0:05 · opening
The hook: "What is wrong with this figure?"
Display a real, confusing figure from an agricultural journal, no context given. Students have 1-2 minutes to jot down what confuses them. Invite 2–3 voices. Do not explain yet, the goal is to surface intuition and build curiosity for what comes next.
0:05 – 0:18 · direct instruction
Six Gestalt principles applied to scientific figures
Proximity · Similarity · Continuity · Closure · Figure-ground · Symmetry. Each principle: brief definition → visual pair (effective vs. ineffective example) → agricultural case (e.g., yield curves, lactation graphs, pest distribution maps, soil profile comparisons).
0:18 – 0:35 · active learning
Figure Clinic — pair work
Handout with three real figures from agricultural journals (one effective, two problematic). In pairs: (a) identify which principles are violated or respected, (b) propose one specific improvement for the most confusing figure. Whole-class debrief: 2–3 pairs share findings; instructor synthesizes recurring patterns.
0:35 – 0:43 · synthesis
A pre-submission checklist for any figure
Slide with five quick questions students can ask themselves before submitting a manuscript figure or giving a presentation. Discuss how good figures signal scientific rigor.
0:43 – 0:50 · closing
Return to the hook + exit ticket
Bring back the opening figure: what do students see now that they did not see before? Exit ticket (index card or short form): "One Gestalt principle I will apply to my next figure, and how." Preview: next class, students bring one of their own figures for a peer clinic.

Materials to develop

Slide deck (~18 slides)
Hook slide · one slide per principle with good/bad pair · activity instructions · pre-submission checklist · closing slide with exit ticket prompt
Figure Clinic handout
Three real figures from agr. journals · analysis table (principle / observation / suggested improvement) · blank space to sketch a revised version of the weakest figure

Sources for figures: Journal of Animal Science, Field Crops Research, Agronomy Journal. Look for figures with unclear legends, low-contrast color schemes, overcrowded panels, or ambiguous axes (to work on the diagnostic exercise).

Figure pre-submission checklist (draft)

  • Is there a clear visual hierarchy: does the eye know where to look first?
  • Do colors group related elements or create unintended clusters?
  • Can the figure be understood without reading the caption first?
  • Is there enough contrast between figure and background?
  • Would someone with color vision deficiency still read this correctly?
Course Syllabus

Scientific Communication in Agricultural Sciences

Level: Mixed undergrad (senior) & grad
Credits: 3 · 14 weeks · 75 min/week
Status: Initial outline

Course description

Scientists working in agricultural fields communicate across multiple registers and for multiple audiences: peer-reviewed journals, extension publications, conference presentations, agency reports, and farmer field days. This course develops the skills needed to navigate those contexts with clarity and confidence. Students learn to write and structure scientific manuscripts, adapt language and visuals for different audiences, critically evaluate primary literature, and present data effectively. All examples and practice materials are drawn from crop science, animal science, soil science, and food systems research.

Learning outcomes

1
Produce a well-structured scientific manuscript following IMRaD format, with discipline-appropriate language and citation practices
2
Adapt scientific content (language, visuals, and level of detail) to at least two distinct audiences (scientific peers and non-specialist stakeholders such as farmers)
3
Design effective data visualizations that apply principles of visual perception and meet standards for scientific publication
4
Deliver a structured oral presentation appropriate to the communicative context (academic conference vs. applied field setting)
5
Navigate the publication and dissemination process - from choosing a journal and responding to peer review, to sharing research through open access, social media, and non-academic formats

Thematic units

Unit 1 · ~3 weeks
The scientific paper as a genre
IMRaD structure and logic · types of papers: research article, review, short communication, technical note, perspective/opinion · how to read a paper strategically · journal conventions across disciplines
Unit 2 · ~3 weeks
Scientific language and register
Hedging and epistemic precision · academic vs. colloquial register · writing for farmers vs. writing for scientists · translating technical content for extension audiences · avoiding jargon without losing accuracy
Unit 3 · ~3 weeks
Data visualization
Gestalt principles · choosing the right graph type for the data · figure design for publication · color, contrast, and accessibility · common errors in agricultural figures · tools and workflows
Unit 4 · ~3 weeks
Oral and multimodal communication
Slide design for conference talks · presenting at farmer field days · scientific posters · handling Q&A from different audiences · communicating uncertainty to non-specialists
Unit 5 · ~2 weeks
Publication and dissemination
Choosing a journal · the peer review process from both sides · writing responses to reviewers · preprints and open access · science beyond the paper: social media, press releases, policy briefs

Assignments and grade distribution

Assignment Description % grade
Paper analysis memo Critical reading of two papers of different types; compare structure, language, and rhetorical choices 15%
Figure revision Redesign a published figure from agricultural literature applying Gestalt principles; include written rationale 15%
Dual-audience piece Communicate the same findings for two audiences: a scientific abstract and a one-page extension summary written for farmers or land managers 20%
Manuscript section draft Write one section (Introduction or Discussion) of a scientific paper on a topic in the student's own research area; includes a peer review round 20%
Oral presentation 12-min talk + Q&A. Students choose their audience (academic conference or field day) and are assessed on content, delivery, and contextual appropriateness 20%
Participation & in-class work Attendance, workshop contributions, and quality of peer feedback given throughout the semester 10%

Graduate students will complete one additional component for the manuscript section draft: a one-page research proposal abstract framed for a funding agency, summarizing their project's objectives, approach, and potential impact for a non-journal audience.

About me

I am a PhD student in Soil and Crop Sciences with a background in agricultural research and a persistent interest in how scientific ideas travel: from the lab or the field to the people who need them most. I am originally from Argentina, where I learned early that science looks and sounds different depending on who is in the room. That experience shapes how I think about communication: not as a fixed skill to master, but as a practice of paying attention to your audience and making deliberate choices about how to reach them.

I bring a creative approach to my own scientific work, and I believe that creativity and rigor are not opposites, the most effective scientific communicators are both precise and imaginative. In this course, I hope to model that balance and help you find your own voice as a scientist.

Diversity and inclusion statement

Science is not produced in a vacuum, and neither is scientific communication. Who gets to speak, in what language, in what format, and for which audience, these are not neutral questions. In this course, we will work with those questions deliberately.

Students come to this course from different disciplinary backgrounds, native languages, research contexts, and relationships with agriculture. That diversity is not an obstacle to navigate, it is one of the most valuable resources we have. A student who grew up on a farm and a student who has only worked in a lab will read the same paper differently, and both readings matter.

I am committed to creating a space where every student can participate fully. That means we will practice communicating for audiences that look like all of us, not just academic English speakers with journal access, and it means I will do my best to make sure course materials, examples, and expectations are transparent and accessible. If something is not working for you, I want to know.

Sections still to develop for the full syllabus

Attendance policy Late work policy Electronics policy Grading scale (letter grades) Accessibility & accommodations Academic integrity
